entries.title: Germany seeks energy and trading partners in Africa
entries.summary: Germany wants to boost relations with Africa. During his trip, German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul has promoted cooperation on energy, infrastructure and the economy. He also criticized US treatment of South Africa.
entries.content:
Africa

Germany seeks energy and trading partners in Africa

July 23, 2026

Germany wants to boost relations with Africa. During his trip, German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul has promoted cooperation on energy, infrastructure and the economy. He also criticized US treatment of South Africa.

In a tent in the Nigerian metropolis of Lagos, two worlds collided: Three fashionable influencers sat on cushioned stools across from Germany's 63-year-old Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul. For half an hour, the senior politician met youth from Africa's creative scene.

These young people are among the stars of Nigeria's creative industry — in a country with a population of around 240 million and a median age of just under 18. Wadephul wanted to understand what moves the younger generation. What issues are on their minds? How do they view Germany? And what do they expect from a collaboration?

After a brief hesitation, they replied: Germany and Europe listen to their music, view their art, and want to collaborate with them. But anyone who wants to travel to the German capital for Fashion Week or the Berlinale film festival can hardly get a visa, said a young man in the audience.

A conversation gets underway; everyone enjoys the exchange and subsequently uploads upbeat videos to social media platforms — to the influencers' channels as well as those of the Federal Foreign Office. This is how German diplomacy is pursuing a direct line of communication with Africa's younger generation.

Nigeria as a valuable energy supplier

There is good reason for the Foreign Minister to be promoting Germany in Nigeria. "The country is an economic powerhouse and, as Africa's largest domestic market, also a key destination for German exports," he says. Nigeria is sub-Saharan Africa's second-largest economy and already imports machinery, chemicals, and food products "Made in Germany."

However, Nigeria's raw materials account for the largest share of the trade balance. They have become even more important since the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. "In June, 25% of jet fuel shipments to Europe came from Nigeria. But we can do significantly more," emphasized Wadephul.

Vital infrastructure support

Wadephul was accompanied by a business delegation on his third trip to Africa. The group includes major corporations like Siemens as well as small and medium-sized enterprises. One of these is VIDA, a Munich-based AI company that is finalizing a multi-million-euro contract with the Nigerian government. The focus is on AI-driven infrastructure planning.

"Our solutions help identify and evaluate new locations. AI allows us to determine much more quickly the economic potential of a site, as well as the local conditions regarding security, climate trends, and other key factors," explained VIDA founder Tobias Engelmeier. His company is already implementing projects in 20 African countries.

German support for infrastructure development is in demand in many places. Since the European Union launched a €290 million ($330 mio) package as part of its Global Gateway strategy, hopes have been rising — both in Africa and among European companies — for faster progress in expanding transport, energy, and utility networks. The Nigerian government is also actively lobbying for German investment to modernize its power supply.

Underestimated economic and political significance

Yet Germany has long underestimated Nigeria's economic and political importance. Given the potential of what is Germany's second-most important trading partner in sub-Saharan Africa, the presence of around 100 German companies is comparatively small. Siemens is involved in expanding the power grid, while Bayer is planning pharmaceutical production facilities together with regional partners. Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) are also increasingly entering the market, though many complain about a lack of transparency in market and decision-making structures.

"It is a challenging market with high operating costs. That is why local partners who understand the diverse structures across the 36 states are essential," says Sabine Dal'Omo, Managing Director of Siemens Sub-Saharan Africa and Chairwoman of the German-African Business Association.

Renewable energy provider Enertrag is among the companies taking a wait-and-see approach. "For us, Nigeria holds great potential — there is strong domestic demand and favorable conditions for exporting green energy. However, the overall framework remains too uncertain for us to commit to a long-term investment," said board member Anne Bendzulla.

Instead, Enertrag has been successfully investing in South Africa for several years. There, the company states it is prepared to invest around €850 million in wind energy.

Missing out by arriving late

The security situation in Nigeria is a matter of concern for German business. Consequently, the federal government has made additional funds available for cooperation with Nigeria's police authorities.

Wadephul urges the German business community not to hesitate too long. "Anyone not present in Africa today risks missing out on crucial opportunities," he told reporters. The country, with its young population, possesses immense potential and abundant resources, he added. "It would be grossly negligent of us to overlook that."

On the final day of his third trip to Africa this year, Wadephul visited South Africa, Germany's most important economic partner on the continent.



At a press conference in the capital Pretoria, South African Minister for International Relations Ronald Lamola praised the fact that "some 600 German companies operate in our country [South Africa] and employ approximately 100,000 people."

In April 2026, the two countries had elevated their relationship to a Strategic Partnership and adopted a Joint Action Plan, which focuses on strengthening cooperation in the areas of trade and investment, security and energy transition. Today, about 80% of South Africa's electricity is still generated from coal.

While in the South African capital, Wadephul criticized US President Donald Trump for not inviting South Africa, which he described as "an indispensable member of the G20," to the group's next summit.

"To exclude South Africa means sidelining an important African voice and a regional economic and political powerhouse," the German foreign minister said. "This can be in no one's interest. As — particularly at a time of growing global economic uncertainty — we need more cooperation, not less!"

He added that he was confident that "South Africa will be able to participate again as a full member of the G20 next year."

He also agreed that African states were underrepresented in the United Nations. "There can be no representative United Nations without permanent African representation on the Security Council," he said.

Wadephul's four-day diplomatic offensive in Africa is evidence of Germany's desire to engage more with democracies and middle powers around the world amid a crisis of multilateralism. As Chinese and Russian influence on the continent increase, Germany with Europe wants to offer an alternative to the Global South.

This article has been translated from German.

entries.title: Sweden eases mining permits to cut China minerals reliance
entries.summary: Sweden has declared the mining of critical minerals and rare earths a national security interest. This will ease and expedite the permit process. The government said the step was needed to counter dependence on China.
entries.content:
Sweden

Sweden eases mining permits to cut China minerals reliance

July 23, 2026

Sweden has declared the mining of critical minerals and rare earths a national security interest. This will ease and expedite the permit process. The government said the step was needed to counter dependence on China.

Sweden on Thursday announced plans to try to boost its mining sector, particularly when it comes to rare earths, and counter European dependence on China. 

Resource-rich Sweden is already home to Europe's largest iron ore mine, and has since found considerable rare earth deposits at the same Arctic Circle site in Kiruna. 

Rare earth minerals are essential for renewable energy generation, defense and electric vehicle production, modern computer manufacturing and other core industries. China accounts for roughly 70% of global mining and refining.

What did the Swedish government say about the plan?

The government in Stockholm on Thursday declared the mining of critical metals and rare earth minerals to be a national security interest.

This should ease and accelerate the permit process for new extraction projects and help attract investments, according to Energy Minister Ebba Busch, who is also deputy prime minister.

"Sweden must become a leading mining nation," she said as she outlined the new strategy, saying the government intended to "streamline authorization procedures so decisions can be taken more quickly."

"We believe that this can contribute significantly to both Sweden's the EU's and NATO's defense capabilities," Busch said, calling European industry's dependence on China a "systemic risk."

Busch also said the move would make it easier for the state to assign land for mining projects, an area of tension with the Sami reindeer-herding indigenous people, who say the expansion of mining could spell the end for their traditional way of life.

The energy minister also said the government was looking into setting up a state-owned investment company for the mining sector.

"Today, to put it plainly, the governemnt lacks concrete tools to wisely contribute to accelerating and attracting investments in crucial strategic projects," she said.

What are Sweden's existing mining credentials?

Sweden has been a European mining giant since the 10th century and the opening of the vast Falun copper mine, which at one point produced as much as two-thirds of the continent's copper. The distinctive deep red color that adorns many houses, churches and other buildings in the country is called Falun Red and hails from the disused mine. It's now a museum and UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Sweden's modern-day Falun is the vast LKAB iron ore mine in Kiruna in the Arctic Circle. Sweden holds around 60% of Europe's known iron ore deposits and, as of 2021, was responsible for around 90% of the continent's iron ore extraction, around one-third of that from Kiruna alone.

The vast frozen mining town made headlines in recent years because of the need to relocate various homes and buildings — including the church — in Kiruna because of subsidence as the giant operation literally undermines the city.

More than 90% of Sweden's mines operate in the sparsely populated and resource-rich Norrland region in Sapmi, the traditional territory of the Sami.

Near the Kiruna mine is the Per Geijer find, a flagship EU project seeking to reduce mineral reliance on China. This is an area where around 1.2 billion tons of mineral resources, including around 2.2 million tons of rare earth oxides, have been identified.

Mining group LKAB is examining the potential of the deposit but estimates that obtaining permits to excavate on Sami land could take 10 years.

Four of the EU's 47 strategic projects aimed at reducing mineral dependence on China are located in Sweden.

Edited by: Sean Sinico

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: US threatens 'major military punishment' for Houthi attacks
entries.summary: President Donald Trump threatened to strike Iran and the Houthi militants over attacks on Saudi tankers. Meanwhile, the UN warned that the situation is 'out of control.' DW has the latest.
entries.content:
Iran

US threatens 'major military punishment' for Houthi attacks

Published July 23, 2026last updated July 23, 2026

President Donald Trump threatened to strike Iran and the Houthi militants over attacks on Saudi tankers. Meanwhile, the UN warned that the situation is 'out of control.' DW has the latest.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Trump threatens 'major military punishment' after Houthi ship attacks
  • US says Saudi nuclear deal depends on kingdom recognizing Israel
  • UN secretary-general says situation in Middle East is 'out of control'
  • A US-Iran ceasefire deal fell apart on July 8
  • Yemen's Houthis attack Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea, say the tankers violated a naval blockade

Follow below for the latest developments on the US war with Iran and news from the wider Mideast on Thursday, July 23:  

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click hereto add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section German military says it will withdraw vessels from Red Sea

German military says it will withdraw vessels from Red Sea

Germany's navy is expected to move two vessels from the Red Sea region to the eastern Mediterranean "in the coming days," a Defense Ministry statement said.

The ministry said it was pulling the ships due to "the volatile political situation... and the absence of any foreseeable near-term start of operations" in the Strait of Hormuz.

The ministry added that keeping the vessels deployed in the region indefinitely "represented a strain on their technical systems."

The two vessels would be "kept in a state of readiness for deployment" and would return to the Middle East "as soon as reliable indicators for an imminent start of the mission are in place," the statement read.

According to the AFP news agency, the ships in question are the "Fulda" minesweeper and the "Mosel" supply ship currently in Djibouti waters.

Skip next section IAEA awaits US-Saudi nuclear deal verification request

IAEA awaits US-Saudi nuclear deal verification request

The International Atomic Energy ⁠Agency (IAEA) said in a statement that while it is aware of a planned US-Saudi request for the agency to carry out verification work ​in relation to the ​nuclear cooperation ​deal, it has not yet received such a request. ‌

The deal discussed Wednesday would allow Saudi Arabia to build nuclear reactors using US technology, to enrich uranium and reprocess nuclear waste.

"We look forward to receiving ​the request for such verification and to ⁠working ​with the ​US and KSA (Kingdom ​of Saudi Arabia) ‌in ensuring the implementation of ​those ⁠measures," the IAEA ​said in a statement, according to Reuters news agency.

Skip next section UN says Middle East situation is 'out of control'

UN says Middle East situation is 'out of control'

UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres warned that the ongoing conflict in the Middle East has pushed the region to the "edge of the unimaginable."

"The situation is getting out of control. It is teetering on the edge of the unimaginable. The region is being pulled into an ever-widening circle of confrontation. One crisis feeds another. One escalation triggers the next," Guterres told a Security Council meeting.

"As this dynamic spreads, the political objectives are becoming increasingly obscured by the confrontation itself," he added.

Skip next section Majority of Gaza at risk of acute hunger

Majority of Gaza at risk of acute hunger

Over two-thirds of people in Gaza are at risk of acute hunger by the year-end due to reduced humanitarian ‌aid flows, the global hunger monitor Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) said.

The IPC said a reduction in fighting and the fact that Israel was allowing more aid into Gaza had a "significant impact, albeit unevenly across areas."

The report also noted that most families living in Gaza were still not getting nearly enough to eat.

"Even small disruptions to humanitarian access could rapidly reverse recent gains," the report warned.

Over 1.2 million people, or about 59% of Gaza's population, faced acute hunger. This includes around 212,000 people in emergency conditions, the IPC said.

That figure is expected to rise ‌to more than 1.4 million people, or 67% by December.

Currently, the majority of people in the Gaza Strip depend on aid supplies.

Israel has rejected the IPC's findings and has consistently denied allegations that it pursued a policy of starvation in Gaza.

Skip next section READ MORE — Oil prices jump as Hormuz, Red Sea crises deepen

READ MORE — Oil prices jump as Hormuz, Red Sea crises deepen

Renewed strikes on shipping in the Gulf and the Red Sea have dealt a fresh double blow to global oil markets, pushing the price of Brent crude to over $100 per barrel for part of Thursday.

As prices climb once again, DW examines what else could keep them elevated.

Skip next section Israel welcomes idea of Saudi Arabia joining Abraham Accords

Israel welcomes idea of Saudi Arabia joining Abraham Accords

The office of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the US-Israeli war against Iran has "the possibility to expand the circle of peace."

It pointed to the prospect of Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords — which has seen some Arab countries normalize relations with Israel — after the Saudis recently signed a nuclear deal with the US.

"As [US] President Donald Trump has said, Saudi Arabia's joining of the Abraham Accords would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East," the Israeli prime minister's office statement said.

Skip next section France calls Houthi strikes 'a serious escelation'

France calls Houthi strikes 'a serious escelation'

France has condemned the Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen for their fresh wave of strikes on Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.

"Houthi attacks on ​Saudi ‌tankers are irresponsible and a serious escalation," a ‌French ‌Foreign Ministry spokesperson told reporters on Wednesday.

With shipping largely halted in the Strait of Hormuz, the Red Sea has served as an alternative export route for Saudi oil. The Houthis announced a maritime blockade in the region earlier this week and claim to have targeted two oil tankers. 

Skip next section Trump threatens 'major military punishment' after Houthi ship attacks

Trump threatens 'major military punishment' after Houthi ship attacks

US President Donald Trump has threatened to strike Yemen and Iran if the Iran-backed Houthi rebels continue to attack ships in the Red Sea.

"A year ago the United States of America attacked, very powerfully, the Houthis, for their interference with commerce and trade, by shooting at ships. Since that time, and during our conflict with Iran, they have acted very responsibly. Unfortunately, now they are starting up again, shooting at two Saudi Arabian ships last night," Trump posted on his Truth Social platform.

"If they do this again, the US will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a surrogate and/or proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis themselves."

Trump added that he was "very disappointed" with the Houthis because "they have, until now, acted very professionally and smart."

Skip next section Trump: Nuclear deal depends on Saudis joining Abraham Accords

Trump: Nuclear deal depends on Saudis joining Abraham Accords

President Donald Trump posted on Thursday that the US-Saudi nuclear deal reached a day ago is contingent on Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords, the bilateral agreements that saw the normalization of diplomatic relations between Israel and some Middle East countries. 

The US president added that the agreement with Saudi Arabia was a "Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)" and that it pertains to non-military uses of nuclear energy.

Trump concluded his post on Truth Social saying, "The United States is not opposed to Civil (Non-Enriched) Nuclear Facilities."

Skip next section EU tells Houthis to 'cease' attacks on Red Sea ships

EU tells Houthis to 'cease' attacks on Red Sea ships

EU top diplomat Kaja Kallas called on Yemen's Houthi rebels to halt attacks on ships in the Red Sea.

"The attack on an oil tanker in the Red Sea is unacceptable, illegal and further inflames an already volatile situation in the region," Kallas said.

"The Houthis must cease all actions that endanger international shipping and the lives of seafarers," she said.

The EU's foreign policy chief added that the bloc's naval mission to the Red Sea "remains firmly committed to protecting freedom of navigation for all."

The statement comes after the Iran-aligned militant group said that it had attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.

Days earlier, the Houthis had announced a blockade of Saudi ports in the Red Sea

Skip next section Jordan says it intercepted Iranian missiles, drones

Jordan says it intercepted Iranian missiles, drones

The Jordanian military said it had intercepted a salvo of Iranian missiles and drones on Thursday.

Jordan's official Petra news agency cited the army as saying that three missiles and six drones were intercepted, while one missile "fell in a remote uninhabited area."

It said that the barrage had wrought no casualties or material damage.

Iran's Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) acknowledged the attacks on Jordan in a Thursday statement, saying it had destroyed the radar of a US THAAD missile defense system, a Patriot defense system, a C-RAM radar and a helicopter hangar.

The report could not be independently confirmed.

Skip next section WATCH: US and Saudi Arabia announce landmark nuclear deal

WATCH: US and Saudi Arabia announce landmark nuclear deal

The US has signed a deal with Saudi Arabia to help the kingdom develop a civilian nuclear program.

Is this the beginning of a new arms race in the Middle East?

Watch below for more on the topic from DW.

Skip next section Houthis 'snookered' into joining US-Iran conflict — Rubio

Houthis 'snookered' into joining US-Iran conflict — Rubio

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that he believed that Yemen's Houthi rebels had been tricked into joining the conflict with the United States by Tehran.

"The Houthis largely were smart and stayed out of all this throughout the conflict, but they now apparently have gotten themselves suckered into this, going after Saudi Arabia and their ships," Rubio told reporters on the sidelines of a summit of the ASEAN Southeast Asian bloc in Manila, the Philippines.

"I hope they will de-escalate, because I think the Houthis, frankly, got snookered into this thing by the Iranians," he said.

The militant group controls much of Yemen's most populous areas, including the capital, Sanaa, and is allied with Iran. It already began threatening merchant ships in the Red Sea in response to Israel's offensive in Gaza in 2023.

The top US official also accused Iran of going back on its commitments in peace talks.

"These people make deals ‌and then they break ‌it ​almost immediately or they decide, 'oh, let's change the deal'. ​That's not how deals work," he said.

"So now ⁠they're ​paying the ​price for it. And ​maybe they'll change ‌their mind here over the ​next ⁠few days as they continue to ⁠suffer ​great losses," Rubio added.

He said he believed Iran was not yet ready for a deal with Washington but said it might be soon, while also reiterating the US' position that Iran should be "denuclearized." Iran maintains its nuclear program is exclusively for civilian purposes.

Earlier this month, US President Donald Trump declared that a previous ceasefire deal with Iran was "over."

Skip next section WATCH: Shipping crisis expands to Red Sea as Hormuz traffic stands still

WATCH: Shipping crisis expands to Red Sea as Hormuz traffic stands still

Threats from Yemen's Iran-backed Houthis are prompting some vessels to turn around rather than risk transiting the Bab el-Mandeb Strait at the southern end of the Red Sea, expanding the shipping crisis from the Strait of Hormuz.

Watch below for more on the crisis after the collapse in talks between the United States and Iran.

Skip next section Iran says it hit US bases in Kuwait

Iran says it hit US bases in Kuwait

Iran has carried out strikes on US targets in Kuwait, Iran's semi-official Fars news agency reported on Thursday.

Fars cited Iran's Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) as saying that they had attacked Udairi landing zone in northwestern Kuwait, as well as the Ali Al Salem air base that lies nearly 60 kilometers (37.3 miles) west of Kuwait City.

Meanwhile, the Iranian army said that it had attacked the Arifjan base in the south and also the Doha base near the Kuwaiti capital.

Earlier on Thursday, Kuwait's military reported its air defenses had engaged "hostile drones."

entries.title: US-Saudi nuclear deal: How will it impact Middle East, US-Iran war?
entries.summary: Critics say the US-Saudi deal could spark a nuclear arms race in the Middle East. It's certainly not making US allies, like Israel and the UAE, happy. And reports suggest the US may already be backtracking on the deal.
entries.content:
Saudi Arabia

US-Saudi nuclear deal: How will it impact Middle East?

July 23, 2026

Critics say the US-Saudi deal could spark a nuclear arms race in the Middle East. It's certainly not making US allies, like Israel and the UAE, happy. And reports suggest the US may already be backtracking on the deal.

After new statements by US President Donald Trump on Thursday morning, it is still unclear whether a controversial new dealwith Saudi Arabia that allows the Middle Eastern autocracy to establish a civilian nuclear program will go ahead.

Trump said the deal could only progress if Saudi Arabia normalized diplomatic relations with Israel. In the recent past, the Saudi government has repeatedly stated it would only do this if the Israelis make genuine moves toward establishing a Palestinian state. With the current Israeli government, that is unlikely to happen.

The deal must also be voted on by the US Congress and there's been backlash already, with critics saying it could set off a nuclear arms race in the Middle East.

One of the main reasons experts are so worried is that in several years' time, the deal, as it stands now, may let the Saudis enrich their own uranium. The more uranium is enriched, the more it could potentially be diverted to make weapons. And in the past Saudi Arabian ruler Mohammed bin Salman has said that if Iran produces a nuclear bomb, then Saudi Arabia would need to have one too.

The majority of the approximately 38 countries with civilian nuclear energy programs don't enrich uranium. Instead they import it.

The US-Saudi deal also has regional implications, especially because it seems to have been formulated in a far less rigorous way than similar deals.

United Arab Emirates: Preferential treatment for Saudis?

The less strict deal the US has made with Saudi Arabia is likely to upset the United Arab Emirates, who often compete with the Saudis for geopolitical influence.

The UAE already has a nuclear power plant, Barakah, as a result of arrangements made with the Americans back in 2009.

As with almost all of the 26 nuclear cooperation agreements the US has, the UAE's deal also included what's known as an "additional protocol" signed with the International Atomic Energy Agency, or IAEA. This means the IAEA can thoroughly inspect all nuclear facilities. The UAE also agreed to forgo uranium enrichment.

The Saudi deal is bilateral only — that is, just between the US and the Saudis and doesn't include the IAEA — and the Saudis may eventually be allowed to enrich their own uranium.

For the UAE, nuclear power was always more about the prestige of being the first Arab nation to develop it than about military ambitions, Nour Eid told DW earlier this year. Eid is an independent researcher based in Paris who has published papers on nuclear ambitions in the Middle East for a number of think tanks.

But, she pointed out, there is also a clause in the UAE agreement that stipulates that "if a regional or neighboring country has a more flexible agreement then they reserve the right to renegotiate their terms."

Israel: Not only one with nuclear bomb?

The Saudi-US nuclear deal will also worry the Israelis, especially if it leads to the development of a nuclear bomb in the Gulf state. That would make Saudi Arabia the only other country in the Middle East to have one.

Israel has never confirmed having nuclear weapons but it's generally acknowledged that it does, probably around 90 warheads plus delivery systems.

Israeli politicians see the Saudi deal as a threat and say their wishes have been ignored. "Every military nuclear program begins with a civilian one," former Israeli defense minister Avigdor Lieberman warned.

The Saudis have lobbied for their own civilian nuclear program for years and, at one stage, possible US-Saudi cooperation on this was a seen as a foreign policy sweetener: If Saudi Arabia, often perceived as a leader of the Islamic world, agreed to normalize diplomatic relations with Israel, it would get nuclear technology from the US.

Any detachment of the nuclear issue from normalization is unlikely to please the Israelis.

"The agreement — coupled with the prospect of the US selling F-35 fighter jets to Turkey — adds to already existing differences between the US and Israel over Iran, and Israeli occupation of south Lebanon and parts of southern Syria," James Dorsey, an expert on the region at Singapore's Rajaratnam School of International Studies, wrote in his regular newsletter on Thursday.

Why the US might offend its ally

There are a number of possible reasons why the US has come up with this deal.

Paris-based researcher Eid pointed out it may have to do with American competition with China and Russia, the world's main nuclear suppliers. The Chinese and Russians have submitted bids to build nuclear plants in Saudi Arabia and these tend to come with fewer conditions, she said. Trump also previously promised to revitalize the US nuclear sector and the Saudi deal will be worth billions.

There's also the Iran war to consider. The US-Israeli war on Iran, which started late February, saw Iran target US-allied Gulf countries. That has strained American relations with Saudi Arabia, which is caught between Israel and Iran's hegemonic ambitions.

This deal binds the US and Saudi Arabia closer together, says Rosemary Kelanic, director of the Middle East program at US-based think tank, the Defense Priorities Foundation.

For example, it gives "Saudi Arabia a potent source of leverage to extract future concessions from Washington, including new security guarantees, by threatening to weaponize its program unless the US promises enhanced military protection," Kelanic argued in a Wednesday press statement.

The US might feel compelled to trade defense protection for the Saudis giving up potential nuclear weapons, as happened in West Germany and South Korea, she says.

"They made nuclear progress, we wanted to prevent them from getting the bomb, and ultimately we offered them greater US military protection so they'd abandon their programs," Kelanic explained. "Giving Riyadh uranium enrichment could functionally enmesh the US with Saudi Arabia forever."

The deal might also mean the US is forced to intervene to save the Saudi monarchy if it was threatened, she noted.

Iran: Regional rivalry

When the deal was announced, observers were quick to point out the inherent hypocrisy. The Trump administration was now prepared to give Saudi Arabia the very things they were bombing Iran for, various experts argued.

It's a blow to the Iranians because "their sworn enemy [the US] … is giving their regional rival a head start on its own nuclear program," Kelanic said on social media platform, X.

That is why the deal makes a swift end to the current conflict between Iran and the US less likely, she argued. "Iran will definitely be less trusting of the US and less willing to make any concessions," Kelanic noted online.

The deal also makes the Iranians more likely to try and weaponize their own nuclear program faster, she continued.

Other observers agree and also fear the beginning of a nuclear arms race in the Middle East.

However, as Eid pointed out, none of this is likely to happen in the near term. It will take the Saudis somewhere between 10 and 20 years to develop nuclear power, let alone nuclear weapons, she told DW.

Edited by: M. Cavanagh

entries.title: Is Germany investing in the right weapons?
entries.summary: Germany wants to build up its army and pour hundreds of millions of euros into its defenses. But in a world where the battlefield is increasingly dominated by cheap drones, how many expensive tanks does the country need?
entries.content:
Germany

Is Germany investing in the right weapons?

July 23, 2026

Germany wants to build up its army and pour hundreds of millions of euros into its defenses. But in a world where the battlefield is increasingly dominated by cheap drones, how many expensive tanks does the country need?

The German government's efforts to modernize and expand its military, the Bundeswehr, is in danger of being derailed by the country's slow procurement processes, according to critics. 

The wars in Ukraine and Iran have shown that drones costing just tens of thousands can be an effective against weapons that devoured countless millions to develop and manufacture. Ukrainian air defenses, for example, have shown that they are able to hold their own against Russian attacks despite the fact that the Ukraine air force has only a fraction of the number of fighter jets and bombers. 

Meanwhile, Germany has massively boosted its defense spending in the past few years. On top of the €100 billion ($114 billion) "special fund" announced in the wake of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the state has been rapidly raising its regular defense spending: From €62.4 billion in 2025 to €82.7 billion in 2026, with plans to raise that to €152 billion by 2029.

But critics are wondering whether these vast sums are being spent in the best way, not least since the FCAS Franco-German fighter jet, which cost over €100 billion, was scrapped in June. 

Defense analyst Nico Lange, who once worked for the German Defense Ministry, gave a scathing verdict on the current strategy. "It's the same dysfunctional system as before. They're just pouring significantly more money into it," he told the news portal Web.de. "The point is: Unless you actually change the structures of military planning and procurement, nothing better will come of it."

Germany learning its procurement lessons

Germany is currently climbing a steep learning curve, according to some politicians. Andreas Schwarz, Social Democrat member of the Bundestag defense committee, said the war in Ukraine is "completely changing how war is being fought."

On a visit to Ukraine earlier this month, Schwarz found that the debates that had dominated German politics in the first year after Russia's full-scale invasion of the country — about whether Germany should send Taurus rockets or Leopard tanks to help Ukraine — had become entirely irrelevant.

"When I was in Ukraine last week and visited the Defense Ministry, no one asked me about a Taurus cruise missile or a Leopard tank," he told DW. "What they need is help with drones, with ammunition, with modernizing their IT sector, with satellites and communication."

Can an army still fight with 'legacy' weapons?

But the Ukrainian experience doesn't mean that heavy weapons systems — often called "legacy systems" have become obsolete, as some analysts have been arguing in recent months. Max Becker, policy fellow at the German Council on Foreign Relations (DGAP), said that the Ukrainian war had shown, on the contrary, that tanks or artillery systems were still important to capture and hold territory, as well as for long-range strikes.

"It's about having the right mix of legacy systems, which you still need, and drone technology," Becker told DW. "That is a dilemma because, on the one hand, you still need to invest in very expensive systems like combat tanks, which take a long time from order to delivery, but you also have to invest in systems like drones and loitering munitions."

The Ukrainian military, Becker added, had developed its drone-based strategy partly out of necessity, because of its lack of conventional artillery and combat tanks. "I think the scenarios that Germany and NATO are preparing for are very different from those of the war between Russia and Ukraine because NATO has very different equipment."

Andreas Schwarz also thinks that the Bundeswehr still needs the old weapons. "I don't want to say that we don't need tanks anymore — not at all, we're always going to need them," he said. "But the question is: Do we still need them in that quantity? And could I not complement or even optimize them with smaller, more flexible units?"

Drones are not only becoming more sophisticated, but also easier and cheaper to control — whereas expensive special computer systems were vital in the past, some military drones can now be steered with apps on a smartphone. Schwarz believes that though the German government and defense industry have grasped this new reality, they still need some time to implement the necessary measures to deal with it.

The procurement problem

The devil is in the detail — or, as so often, in the bureaucracy. Defense procurement is notoriously slow and complicated, and has proved especially so in a country where the arms industry is dominated by major companies such as Rheinmetall that have made a lot of money building tanks and submarines for the world's militaries.

"We have an arms industry that for decades has relied on certain tried-and-tested technologies," said Schwarz. "Of course, we also have an army that has spent decades preparing for scenarios that are no longer very likely today. And of course, it’s difficult to establish this new way of thinking when we’ve been operating on the basis of different scenarios up until now — which requires those in charge to be willing to rethink their approach."

In an attempt to speed things up, the German government introduced a new law at the beginning of the year, whose name is as self-explanatory as it is unwieldy: the Bundeswehrbeschaffungsbeschleunigungsgesetz — or, the Military Procurement Acceleration Law.

This measure hopes to decentralize procurement by raising price thresholds so that certain purchases can be made without extra decision-makers being looped in, and removing paperwork for defense procurements between governments.

These are all good things, said Becker, because they help to change the received logic of defense procurement: In a world where drones and weapons can become obsolete within weeks, it makes no sense to make an order and then stockpile weapons that will be outdated in months.

"You create this new logic by agreeing innovation clauses in the procurement process, which obligate the companies to update their systems," Becker said. Seeing that the German government had grasped that such innovations were important had given him hope, he added.

What Germany should be concentrating on, he suggested, was setting up a new manufacturing infrastructure that can react quickly to new orders. "I can't buy a million drones," he said. "I need a stockpile of drones for training, and for exercise purposes, but I also need the industrial capacities so that the production of a mass of drones can be set up within four to six weeks."

The German military is also in the middle of a massive recruitment drive that is proving unpopular among young people — but, despite the increased automation of warfare, Schwarz believes that new human recruits are still essential.  "We still need those people as drone pilots for example, but we also need people who can work with this technology," he said. Not only that, he added, new recruits were necessary to strengthen the reserve army to protect German territory for the worse case scenario.

Edited by: Rina Goldenberg

entries.title: US strikes on southern Iran infrastructure widen war
entries.summary: The latest US strikes on tunnels, bridges and other infrastructure further inland from the Strait of Hormuz could indicate that Washington is shifting gears in Iran.
entries.content:
Asia

US strikes on southern Iran infrastructure widen war

July 23, 2026

The latest US strikes on tunnels, bridges and other infrastructure further inland from the Strait of Hormuz could indicate that Washington is shifting gears in Iran.

With the US and Iran back at war following a prolonged but shaky truce, the latest US strikes seem to be less focused on Iran's coastal installations and maritime targets in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iranian media and local officials have reported strikes on bridges, tunnels, roads, rail infrastructure, power facilities and desalination plants in southern Iran over the past week.

Hormozgan province, just north of the Strait of Hormuz, has been hit especially hard.

According to Iranian media, six bridges in Khamir county were struck early on Friday, July 17.

Hours later, more reports followed of attacks on two tunnels and two more bridges in Hormozgan. At least eight people were reported killed in the bridge strikes.

US campaign aimed at mobility and recovery

The US military also appears to have conducted the strikes in a very deliberate pattern. In the first wave, bridges west and north of provincial capital Bandar Abbas were hit.

In a second wave, another bridge to the east, one to the north and two tunnels north of the city were damaged. Alongside that, part of the railway west of Bandar Abbas was also struck.

Shukria Bradoost, an international security analyst at Virginia Tech in the US, told DW that the main US objective remains the same as before.

"The American goal in attacking southern Iran is to reopen the Strait of Hormuz," she said.

However, she noted that Washington is trying to do more than remove direct military threats to shipping.

The US military is destroying the infrastructure that could help Iran rebuild, reposition or sustain military pressure in the area, she added, noting that this was the reason that bridges, roads and rail lines have become targets.

She also warned that Washington could move to take control the strategic Kharg Island, the home of Iran's main oil export terminal, in its push to remove the wider threat to Hormuz.

Can US cripple Kharg Island without destroying it?

The US has so far refrained from targeting energy facilities on the island, home to Iran's main oil export terminal, said Ahmad Vakhshiteh, director of the Moscow-based Center for Strategic Research on Developing Relations with the Middle East.

"Naturally, the island is valuable as long as its oil facilities remain operational and undamaged," he told DW. "But the key question is whether the United States is seeking to seize Kharg militarily or whether, through repeated attacks, it is pursuing what could be described as a political seizure of the island."

"In other words, rather than deploying ground forces, it could continue nightly attacks on Kharg and other parts of southern Iran to create a public perception that southern Iran has fallen or that its energy infrastructure can no longer operate," Vakhshiteh added, saying that this could then increase public pressure on the Iranian leaders.

"One possible scenario is that, if Iran does not show flexibility in negotiations with the United States, Washington could increase pressure on Kharg Island in an attempt to reduce Iranian oil exports to near zero," Vakhshiteh said.

However, the Russia-based analyst pointed out that this kind of move would cause a major shock to global energy markets, which could also have serious consequences for the United States and for US President Donald Trump. 

"Overall, however, I do not believe the United States will make the miscalculation of deploying its soldiers to Kharg Island. Doing so would draw it into a war of attrition, requiring enormous resources to hold the island before ultimately forcing it to withdraw," he added.

Washington is upping the cost of war

While Washington and Tehran were able to hammer out a memorandum of understanding in June, Iranian officials have ramped up their rhetoric since the burial of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei two weeks ago.

This includes increasingly common references to revenge, Bradoost said, adding that this signals an intensified ideological imperative of confrontation, thereby making compromise harder.

Iran is also trying to widen the energy crisis and shift the costs of war onto others so that the conflict becomes too expensive to sustain.

In turn, Washington seems increasingly focused on gradually wearing down Tehran's ability to project power, from Iran's naval and logistical capacity to its ability to use energy exports and the Strait of Hormuz as leverage.

Game of political survival

The attacks on Iran's infrastructure in Hormozgan reflect a broader contest over the next phase of the war.

Every collapsed bridge and tunnel affects Iran's ability to move forces towards the Strait of Hormuz, compensate losses and keep pressure on one of the world's most sensitive energy chokepoints.

As long as the war continues, however, the Trump administration will be facing political and economic pressures of its own, limiting the scope of its campaign.

While Tehran faces similar pressures to seek compromise, it also has other considerations.

The Iranian leadership appears to believe that if the threat to oil exports, energy security and the Strait of Hormuz disappears, outside pressure and public displeasure could focus on them, rather than outside enemies.

Seen from this perspective, Iran's strategy becomes less focused on defending territory and more about the regime defending its own political survival.

Edited by: Wesley Rahn

entries.title: Trump's refugee policy: Only white South Africans welcome?
entries.summary: Trump is spending more money on refugees, but it's mostly white South Africans who benefit. People from crisis-stricken countries are being denied entry. Experts say the credibility of US refugee aid has been damaged.
entries.content:
South Africa

Trump's refugee policy: Only white South Africans welcome?

July 23, 2026

Trump is spending more money on refugees, but it's mostly white South Africans who benefit. People from crisis-stricken countries are being denied entry. Experts say the credibility of US refugee aid has been damaged.

The US government cited an "unforeseen emergency" in South Africa as the reason the refugee program would be expanded. However, only white minority South Africans — who are allegedly being persecuted on racial grounds in their country — would be granted refugee status. TheTrump administration has repeatedly claimed that there is an ongoing genocide against white Afrikaners , allegations the South African government strongly denies.

Experts also say there is no statistical evidence of increased crime targeting white farmers. 

For Oscar van Heerden, a research fellow at the Center for African Diplomacy and Leadership at the University of Johannesburg, this is based on a distorted understanding intended to "undermine South Africa's moral authority on the international stage — by equating South Africa with the State of Israel."

Since the start of the war in Gaza, South Africa has been among Israel's harshest critics. In late 2023, Pretoria filed a lawsuit with theInternational Court of Justice (ICJ) in The Hague against the Israeli government over allegations of genocide against the Palestinian population in Gaza.

The case stems from Israel’s response to the October 7, 2023 terrorist attacks carried out by Hamas, which is considered a terrorist organization by the US, the EU and others, and other militant Islamist groups. Israel denies the allegations of genocide and invokes its right to self-defense. It could take years before a final decision is reached.

Trump's idea of South Africa

Essentially, according to van Heerden, US President Donald Trump is saying: How can a country like South Africa bring Israel before the ICJ for alleged genocide against the Palestinians in the Gaza Strip when the South Africans themselves are committing genocide against white South Africans?

"He has this twisted notion in his head that he will convince the world that, after the Mandela years, we [South Africa] no longer have any moral authority in the world," he told DW.

Through an emergency order in 2025, Trump made it possible for members of white minority groups from South Africa to enter and resettle in the US. The US State Department's decision now calls for raising the annual cap on refugee admissionsfor the 2026 fiscal year from 7,500 to 17,500. The additional 10,000 spots are reserved exclusively for white South Africans. The State Department estimates that the expanded program would cost around $100 million (€87 million). 

"This is a clear indication of an attempt to politicize protection," Gustavo De Carvalho, an expert on African governance and diplomacy at the South African Institute of International Affairs (SAIIA), told DW.

"The narrative that white South Africans are being persecuted in South Africa has resonated strongly even within the far-right movement in the United States," De Carvalho added.

"We have a crime problem in South Africa that is caused by the country's vast social inequality and poverty," van Heerden said, stressing that all segments of the population had been affected.

US-South Africa ties under strain

According to De Carvalho, South Africa's case against Israel before the International Court of Justice and the country's alleged closeness to countries like Russia and Iran, has caused great confusion and uncertainty in its relationship with the US.

Diplomatic tensionsbetween the two countries are running high. The US government's allegations of genocide against the white minority population are just one piece of the puzzle.

Trump's administration has also cut aid to South Africa, boycotted last year's G20 summit in Johannesburg, and excluded South Africa from this year's G20 conference, which will take place at one of Trump's resorts in Miami in December 2026.

South Africa's position among theBRICS nations also plays a role in Trump's foreign policy.

"BRICS members are talking about decoupling from the dollar and trading in local currencies instead of the US dollar," van Heerden said. "They have established a new development bank to compete with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank. They are also moving toward multilateralism and away from a unipolar world dominated by the US." 

He added that the US cannot engage in a direct confrontation with China, Russia or India; the major BRICS partners. That is why, van Heerden says, it is targeting smaller partners like South Africa to impose punitive measures.

White South African refugees welcome, others not so much

While white South Africans are welcomed in the US, Washington is suspending its resettlement program for people fleeing war and persecution in countries such as Afghanistan, the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Sudan.

According to De Carvalho, this policy "seriously violates a number of principles surrounding the concept of refugee protection" when ethnic criteria are the deciding factor rather than the risk to which people are exposed.

"The US refugee policy is certainly not immigration policy," De Carvalho noted, adding that it was an attempt to gain influence.

In the fiscal year 2024, which began under then-President Joe Biden, the US admitted more than 100,000 refugees, about one-third of whom were from Africa, including many Congolese refugees.

Trump's refugee policy looks different. Out of the roughly 6,700 refugees the US admitted between October 2025 and May 2026, all but three came from South Africa.

"He doesn't care about Africa, nor does he care about refugees," said van Heerden.

The US is in a period of fiscal consolidation, and Donald Trump has to make cuts — which he does, to aid programs, charities and the civil service.

"They have to rein in costs because America spends too much money on others and not on its own citizens; after all, MAGA Make America Great Again (MAGA) is all about 'America First,'" said van Heerden.

De Carvalho, however, said it could backfire on the US: "This selective humanitarianism significantly undermines the US's ability to present itself as a global moral authority."

This article was originally written in German

entries.title: Man dies of injuries after attack at Regensburg bank: police
entries.summary: Police say one person has died after sustaining severe injuries at a bank branch in Regensburg. Special units were dispatched and apprehended a suspect armed with a knife.
entries.content:
July 23, 2026

Police say one person has died after sustaining severe injuries at a bank branch in Regensburg. Special units were dispatched and apprehended a suspect armed with a knife.

Police said on Thursday that a man had died after being critically wounded in an apparent hostage situation at a bank branch in Regensburg, Bavaria. 

The man was taken to a local hospital but was pronounced dead soon after.

A major police operation, including special units and a helicopter, had gathered near the scene on Thursday afternoon.

What else do we know about the standoff?

Police said they were investigating "a homicide as well as suspected attempted robbery," in an update later on Thursday.

"Two people were still inside the building. Special units were able to bring them out of the building in the mean time," police said.

At around 6 p.m. local time, around three hours after the first emergency calls reached police, authorities said that the suspect had been apprehended.

He was identified as a German national aged 20 and was arrested without injury, police said. 

"The police operations on site continue and are not yet complete. Among other things the building is still being searched at present," police said.

The male suspect was believed to be armed with a knife and was thought to be acting alone.

Police repeatedly asked members of the public to steer clear of the operation area.

Earlier updates from law enforcement suggested the motive of the suspect was not known and that no demands had been issued to police.

Regensburg is a city of around 150,000 people located roughly between Nuremberg and Munich in Bavaria, not far from the border to the Czech Republic. The bank branch is located in the west of the city, near the river Danube.

Edited by: Sean Sinico

If you count on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Can India's 'Cockroach' protest movement go nationwide?
entries.summary: What began as satire on social media has turned into a massive Gen Z-led protest movement driven by anger over corruption and high youth unemployment. There are signs it is finding traction outside of Delhi.
entries.content:
India

Can India's 'Cockroach' protest movement go nationwide?

July 23, 2026

What began as satire on social media has turned into a massive Gen Z-led protest movement driven by anger over corruption and high youth unemployment. There are signs it is finding traction outside of Delhi.

A youth-led protest movement that calls itself the "Cockroach Janta Party (CJP)" is beginning to find supporters beyond the Indian capital, after tens of thousands of young demonstrators marched on Parliament in New Delhi early this week.

The youth led Cockroach movement began in May after Indian Supreme Court Chief Justice Surya Kant publicly referred to unemployed young people as "cockroaches" in an offhand remark during an unrelated court hearing.

Young people embraced the moniker, which has become a banner for young people's anger over what they see as failings in the country's education system and its massive youth unemployment problem.

The protests are putting public pressure on Prime Minister Narendra Modi's Hindu nationalist Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP).

And solidarity protests have sprung up beyond New Delhi across India as support for the campaign continues to grow.

BJP takes notice

Frustrations began to mount during a campaign calling for accountability over a scandal involving a highly competitive university entrance exam. The news that the question sheet had been leaked ahead of the exam date in early May exposed long-running grievances over privileged access for some students.

Fueled by millions of young followers on social media, the movement soon tapped into wider frustrations over unemployment, shrinking opportunities and an education system many young Indians believe has failed them.

Until recently, however, many in the ruling BJP dismissed the Cockroach movement as little more than a social media phenomenon.

That changed after activist Sonam Wangchuk staged a three-week hunger strike, and  police launched a crackdown in response to this week's march to India's parliament.

Images of tear gas, baton charges and injured students spread rapidly online, prompting solidarity rallies across India and forcing the government onto the political defensive.

Among other things, protesters are demanding Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan step down.

In a response on X, Pradhan promised "answers, reforms and accountability."

India's main opposition leader, Rahul Gandhi, said on Wednesday that he backed the students' demands, including Pradhan's resignation and an apology to students from Modi.

'Cockroaches' draw national attention

While Delhi remains the nerve center of the movement, rallies and marches have been staged in several other states.

Anjali Bhardwaj, a social activist and transparency campaigner, said the movement was no longer confined to the capital.

Bhardwaj told DW that "the contagion has spread even to remote areas of central Chhattisgarh," a rural state in central India. "The anger is palpable. It has tapped into the frustration and disillusionment of young people who are demanding accountability."

The western state of Maharashtra has emerged as the movement's strongest base outside of Delhi. In the city of Pune, thousands gathered at Savitribai Phule Pune University in June, where CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke unveiled the movement's education manifesto.

In the finance hub of Mumbai, Maharashtra's biggest city, police issued restrictions on public gatherings until August 6 after solidarity demonstrations in support of the CJP.

Authorities there also opened eight criminal cases involving around 900 protesters, citing intelligence reports that warned of possible violence and threats to public order. Security was also reinforced across the city to deter further protests.

"If we walk away now after everything we've endured, it will all have been for nothing," Manthan, the CJP's Maharashtra coordinator, told DW.

The movement's supporters have also begun organizing demonstrations in Bengaluru, Kolkata, Guwahati, Shillong, Dimapur, Hyderabad, Ahmedabad, Thiruvananthapuram, Jammu and parts of Uttar Pradesh.

Farmers' groups side with CJP

In several states, support appears to be driven by students, local activists and civil society groups rather than formal structures run from the center.

"The issues raised by the CJP cannot be dismissed. Examination paper leaks, corruption and unemployment are genuine concerns for young people, and it is natural that students are identifying with the movement," M Sivaprasad, Kerala state president of the Students' Federation of India, told DW.

The movement has also won support from sections of India's farmers' organizations.

"The students and the youth are also the children of farmers and the future of India. SKM firmly believes that the struggles of students and youth are the struggle of farmers," leaders associated with the Samyukta Kisan Morcha (SKM), which led the large-scale protests that succeeded in overturning a series of controversial farming legislation, wrote in a statement. 

However, BJP spokesperson Tom Vadakkan dismissed suggestions that the movement could quickly develop into a nationwide political force, arguing that it faces significant structural obstacles outside the capital.

"The CJP has to bridge India's urban rural divide and overcome language barriers before it can claim to be a national movement," Vadakkan told DW.

"I also don't think the name 'Cockroach' is acceptable in civil society. It may generate attention in Delhi, but I don't see it gaining traction across the states," he said.

Can the movement sustain its momentum?

Political scientist Gilles Verniers from the Paris-based public research university Sciences Po, believes the movement has the potential to spread beyond Delhi because its demands resonate with a much wider audience than those directly affected by examination paper leaks.

"In the face of escalating state repression, the Cockroach movement is likely to expand and spread to other cities," Verniers told DW.

"Its demands are simple enough to mobilize supporters without centralized leadership or coordination. They are also broadly nonpartisan, focusing on the fairness of public institutions and government accountability rather than partisan politics," he added.

Verniers said the movement had the potential to draw in activists and civil society groups that have had few opportunities for sustained public protest since the anti-citizenship law demonstrations of 2019.

Media and political commentator Pamela Philipose believes the movement has already crossed an important threshold.

"It has already built support across the country, judging by the resurgence of youth-led protests in cities across India," she told DW. "That points to a commonality of concerns among young people. By responding with callousness and brutality, the government is inadvertently creating unity among disparate groups that might otherwise have taken years of activism to come together."

The challenge facing the CJP has moved beyond attracting attention towards converting solidarity into sustained organization.

Many protest movements in India have drawn national attention before fading once the immediate trigger passed.

Whether the Cockroach movement follows that pattern or develops into a broader nationwide campaign will depend on its ability to build local leadership, sustain mobilization and convince young people outside Delhi that it speaks to their own aspirations as much as those of students in the capital.

Edited by: Wesley Rahn

entries.title: Spain's solar eclipse trilogy: spectacle starting August 12
entries.summary: From August 2026 to January 2028, amateur and pro skywatchers are in for a series of rare solar spectacles over Spain and North Africa. Astronomers expect the events to attract millions from around the world.
entries.content:
ScienceGlobal issues

Spain's solar eclipse trilogy: spectacle starting August 12

July 23, 2026

From August 2026 to January 2028, amateur and pro skywatchers are in for a series of rare solar spectacles over Spain and North Africa. Astronomers expect the events to attract millions from around the world.

When Josep Masalles was 17 years old, he was captivated by a partial eclipse of the sun over Barcelona, where he lives in northeastern Spain. It sparked a lifelong fascination that has taken him around the world. In the 53 years that have followed, Masalles has witnessed 43 eclipses, including 22 total solar eclipses. 

"The beauty of an eclipse is incomparable," Masalles told DW at the headquarters of the Barcelona Astronomical Society, or ASTER, where he is president. "For me, it has also meant a way to travel, to see other societies and cultures, and to meet other astronomers."

Masalles, along with other astronomers, is now preparing for a rare trio of solar eclipses over Spain and North Africa — Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Egypt — between August 2026 and January 2028. 

What you can expect from the trio of solar eclipses

On August 12, a total eclipse will pass from Galicia in the northwest of Spain to the Balearic Islands in the east, as well as Greenland and the western edge of Iceland.

A second total eclipse will follow on August 2, 2027, visible from the tip of southern Spain, North Africa, and parts of the Middle East.

And in January 2028, an annular eclipse will pass from southwest to northeast Spain.

During an annular eclipse, the moon is too far from the Earth to fully cover the sun — so, it is not a total eclipse — but it does produce a visible "ring of fire," where only the sun's outer edge can be seen.

There are two to three solar eclipses every year. But most happen over the ocean or sparsely populated parts of the Earth. Total solar eclipses tend only to occur every 18 months. 

A total solar eclipse happens when the moon completely blocks the sun's light, casting a region of our planet into darkness.

For Spain to see three solar eclipses, including two total solar eclipses, within roughly a year-and-a-half is exceptional, said Sandra Benitez Herrera, head of science communication and education at the European Space Agency (ESA). 

"This is actually very, very unusual," said Benitez Herrera. "The configuration of the sun-Earth-moon system and the inclination of the orbits has allowed for Spain to have this trio."

A total eclipse hasn't been seen from the Iberian Peninsula since 1912, and after 2027 the next one to pass over any part of Europe won't be until 2053.

At the moment of "totality" — when the moon fully eclipses the sun — the sky darkens, and the outer layers of the sun's atmosphere — known as the corona — become visible to the naked eye.

Totality usually only lasts a few minutes, but a solar eclipse "leaves no one indifferent," said Masalles. "In the face of that beauty, of the grandeur of the universe, it's also a feeling of how small the planet is and how vast the universe is."

A total solar eclipse is a 'magical moment'

Total eclipses let scientists study the sun's corona — its atmosphere — and its effect on the Earth. It's a field called space weather and has become increasingly important, the more we depend on satellites for communication, financial transactions and Earth Observation to monitor climate change. 

Space weather includes solar winds and storms, which can disrupt those satellites and even power grids on Earth.

By combining data from ground-based observations with data from space probes, like ESA's Proba-3 spacecraft, which creates artificial solar eclipses in space, or its Solar Orbiter, scientists hope to calculate the risks of space weather.

But perhaps the greatest impact of solar eclipses on everyday life is educational. They inspire millions of people to learn about astronomy and other aspects of science.

"Even the smallest towns are eager to receive visitors, have star parties and celebrations with music and concerts," said Benitez.

There will be hundreds of public events across Spain along the eclipse path this August. Even months before the first of the three eclipses, you could sense that scientists were excited to share their enthusiasm.

"I've seen people cry. I've seen people laugh. I've seen people doing strange things because it's a very magical moment," said Valentin Juan Martinez Pillet, director of the Institute of Astrophysics of the Canary Islands (IAC), Spain's largest astronomy institution.

The eclipse of the century: a collaboration across continents

Many people are already looking ahead to the 2027 eclipse. It's been dubbed "the eclipse of the century" because the moment of totality is predicted to last more than 6 minutes at its peak over Egypt.

Unlike the 2026 eclipse, which occurs close to sunset, the 2027 eclipse will take place in the late morning, with the sun high in the sky.

A citizen science project led by IAC, in collaboration with Mohammed VI Polytechnic University in Morocco and the US National Solar Observatory (NSO), is laying the groundwork for observing that eclipse.

The North African Telescope Eclipse project (NATE) aims to bring together scientists and high school students from Spain, Morocco and the US. They will get a chance to observe the corona from ten telescope locations across northern Morocco. 

"This means a lot to me," said Youssef Moulane, an astrophysicist at Mohammed VI Polytechnic University.

Moulane, who grew up in a small village near Marrakesh, said that as a young boy his community was afraid of eclipses, fearing they could be dangerous.

"They told us to hide inside, because they did not understand what was going on," he said. "Now, we are doing the inverse. We are inviting people to go outside and watch the eclipse, to put on glasses and enjoy it."

Images will be gathered from each of the locations in Morocco and stitched together to create an extended picture of the sun's atmospheric activity. 

There was a similar project for the 2017 and 2024 total eclipses over the US.

"I'd like to see a coronal mass ejection, a big flare happening when we are observing the sun from Morocco," said Martinez, who ran the original US experiment. "In any case, there will be some science, but mostly it's about attracting and getting the students enthusiastic about science."

A smaller group of students will travel to northern Spain for the 2026 eclipse as a training exercise for the main event in 2027.

Edited by: Zulfikar Abbany
